required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of craftsmen and makers

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Any age-appropriate biography of a builder, maker, or craftsperson

competence_markers.description: They clean and put away tools after every use without being reminded — this is automatic, not prompted
They use materials respectfully — no habitual waste, no careless damage, no treating materials as disposable
They finish projects completely — including cleanup of workspace, return of tools, and restoration of shared spaces
They can describe what "done" looks like for a task they are working on — and their description includes cleanup and tool care, not just the product itself
They demonstrate genuine pride in well-made work — not performative pride for adult approval, but the real internal satisfaction of honest completion
They can identify the difference between careful work and careless work — in their own output and in examples they see
They care for shared spaces without being asked — leaving rooms, workspaces, and common areas in acceptable or improved condition
They engage in show-and-tell conversations about their work honestly — naming what went well and what was hard, without defensiveness

narration_prompts.prompt: What tools do you use most often?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you take care of them? What happens when someone does not take care of a tool?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about something you made that you are proud of.
narration_prompts.detail: What made it good? How did you feel when it was done?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to "finish" a project?
narration_prompts.detail: Is it done when the thing is made, or is there more to it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you ever done something carelessly and then wished you had been more careful?
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do we clean up after we work?
narration_prompts.detail: Is it just a rule, or is there a reason that matters?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone from a story who made something with great care.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they make? How could you tell they cared about it?

cross_connections.description: Craftsmanship is integrity expressed through work. Producing careless work when you are capable of better is a form of dishonesty — a gap between what you can do and what you chose to do. IC1.3 builds the character; IC2.5 shows it in action.

IC2.2 establishes the standard of "do it right." IC2.5 adds the moral dimension — doing it right because you care, not just because the standard requires it. The two are deeply intertwined and should be developed concurrently.
The primary physical arena where IC2.5 principles are practiced. When the student begins formal trades work, the tool care and material respect habits from this packet transfer directly.

Art-making is one of the earliest contexts where craftsmanship principles apply. The care for materials, the attention to finishing, and the honest pride in completed work all appear in the arts domain.

The concept of leaving spaces better than you found them — a core IC2.5 practice — scales directly into civic responsibility. The child who cares for shared spaces at home becomes the citizen who cares for shared spaces in the community.

content.why_this_matters:
Everything you make tells the truth about you.

A birdhouse with crooked nails and splintered edges says something about the person who built it. A drawing left half-colored says something too. A garden with tools thrown on the ground instead of put away — that says something. Not because anyone is judging you. Because the work itself is honest in a way that words are not. You can say "I tried my best" while looking at a mess that proves otherwise. The mess does not lie.

This packet is about learning to care — about the things you make, the tools you use, the spaces you leave behind, and the way you finish what you start. It sounds simple. It is. But simple is not the same as easy, and most grown-ups never learned this. They leave messes. They use tools carelessly and wonder why nothing works right. They stop working the moment something gets hard or boring, and they call it "done" when it is really just "abandoned."

You are going to learn something different. You are going to learn that the last step of any job matters as much as the first. That a tool deserves to be cleaned and put away, not because someone will yell at you, but because that is what a person who respects their work does. That finishing neatly — the cleanup, the put-away, the final check — is not extra. It is part of the work.

And here is the secret that most people never discover: **when you do work you are genuinely proud of, something changes inside you.** Not pride like bragging. Pride like standing next to something you made and knowing — honestly, without anyone having to tell you — that you did it right. That feeling is the foundation of everything this packet builds toward. It is the beginning of craftsmanship.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Care for tools** — treating the things you work with as things that matter, not disposable objects
2. **Respect for materials** — not wasting, not damaging, not treating what you are working with carelessly
3. **Finishing neatly** — understanding that the end of the work includes the cleanup, the put-away, the restoration of order
4. **Pride in honest work** — the internal experience of having made something real and done it well
5. **Tool care as non-negotiable habit** — the work is not done until the tools are put away properly
6. **Beginning to understand that work speaks** — that what you produce says something about you, whether you intend it to or not
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Tools Are Not Toys

A tool is something that helps you do work. A hammer drives nails. A paintbrush puts paint where you want it. A shovel moves dirt. A pencil makes marks. Every tool was made for a purpose, and every tool works best when it is treated with respect.

**What respect for tools looks like:**

- You pick up a tool when you need it and put it back when you are done — in the right place, in the right condition.
- You do not throw tools, drop tools, or leave tools on the ground where they can be stepped on or rained on.
- You clean tools after using them. A paintbrush gets washed. A saw gets wiped. A shovel gets the dirt knocked off before it goes back in the shed.
- You do not use a tool for something it was not made for. A screwdriver is not a chisel. A hammer is not a lever for prying things apart (unless it is designed to be). Using a tool wrong damages the tool and produces bad work.

**Why this matters beyond the tool itself:**

A person who does not take care of their tools is a person who does not take care of their work. The two are connected. If you cannot be trusted with a hammer, you cannot be trusted with a bigger project. If you leave your paintbrushes crusted with dried paint, your next painting will suffer — not because of your skill, but because of your carelessness.

Taking care of tools is the first promise you make as someone who works: **I will treat the things that help me do my work as though they matter.** Because they do.

**For the youngest students (4-5):** Start with a few tools. A small set of crayons or colored pencils. A garden trowel. A dustpan and brush. Learn to use each one, clean it, and put it back. The set is small so the habit can be total — every tool, every time.

**For older Stretching Branches students (6-9):** The tool set expands. Kitchen tools. Workshop tools (with supervision). Art supplies. The principle remains the same, but the expectation grows: you are responsible for more tools, and you maintain them without being reminded each time.

### Part 2: Respecting Materials

Materials are what you work with to make things. Wood, paper, paint, clay, fabric, food, soil — whatever you are using to produce something. Materials deserve respect for a simple reason: they are real, they cost something, and they do not come back once they are wasted.

**What respect for materials looks like:**

- You do not waste. If you need one sheet of paper, you take one sheet — not five because you might mess up. If you mess up, you turn it over and use the other side, or you set it aside for scrap.
- You do not damage materials carelessly. You do not crumple paper for fun, pour out paint you will not use, or break sticks you do not need.
- You use what you have before you ask for more. The crayon that is short still works. The piece of wood that has a knot in it can still be used — you work around the knot.
- You treat the thing you are making as something that matters. If you are building a birdhouse, the birdhouse is real. Birds may live in it. It is not a throwaway exercise — it is a thing in the world that you are responsible for.

**The deeper lesson:**

Wasting materials is a small form of dishonesty. It says: "This does not matter." But it does. Someone grew that wood. Someone made that paper. Someone earned the money that bought that paint. When you use materials carefully, you are honoring the chain of work that brought them to you. When you waste them, you are saying that none of that matters.

This is not about being stingy or afraid to use things. It is about being intentional. Use what you need. Use it well. Do not use more than you need, and do not treat what you use as though it is worthless.

### Part 3: Finishing Neatly — The Last Step Is Part of the Work

Most people think the work is done when the thing is made. The birdhouse is nailed together — done. The drawing is colored in — done. The garden row is planted — done.

It is not done.

**The work includes:**
- Cleaning your workspace
- Putting your tools away — clean, in the right place
- Putting leftover materials back where they belong
- Sweeping up, wiping down, restoring the space to how you found it — or better

This is not punishment added onto the end of something fun. This is the last part of the work itself. A carpenter who builds a beautiful cabinet but leaves sawdust all over the shop floor and tools scattered across the bench has not finished. A painter who creates a lovely picture but leaves paint-soaked brushes hardening on the table has not finished. A cook who makes a delicious meal but leaves the kitchen destroyed has not finished.

**The standard is: the work is done when the space is ready for the next person — or for you, tomorrow.**

This habit, practiced hundreds of times with small tasks, becomes a permanent part of how you operate. The person who finishes neatly at age 7 becomes the person who leaves every workspace, every campsite, every shared space better than they found it. The person who never learns this becomes someone other people have to clean up after — for the rest of their life.

### Part 4: Pride in Honest Work

There is a specific feeling that comes from doing something well. Not perfectly — well. Honestly. Completely. You know it when you feel it. It is not the same as someone telling you "good job." It is deeper than that. It is the feeling of standing next to something you made and knowing, inside yourself, that you did it right.

**This feeling cannot be manufactured.** It only comes from work that was actually done well. You cannot feel proud of something you rushed through. You cannot feel proud of something you half-finished. You cannot feel proud of something you did carelessly. The pride is the natural result of care, effort, and completion. It arrives on its own when the conditions are real.

**What honest work looks like:**
- You tried your best — not a performance of trying, but actual effort applied to the task
- You did not cut corners — you did each step the way it should be done, not the fastest way
- You finished completely — including cleanup
- If something was not right, you went back and fixed it — not because someone told you to, but because you could see it was not right

**What honest work is NOT:**
- Perfection. A five-year-old's birdhouse will not look like a master carpenter's. That is fine. The question is not "is it perfect?" The question is "did you do your honest best with the skills you have right now?"
- Someone else's approval. The feeling of honest pride comes from inside. It is nice when someone notices your work, but the pride is not dependent on that. The work was good because it was good — not because someone said so.

**Why this matters for everything that comes later:**

The child who learns to feel genuine pride in honest work has an internal compass. They know the difference between work they are proud of and work they are not. That compass guides them for the rest of their lives — in school, in trades, in professions, in relationships, in everything they build. The child who never develops this compass relies on other people to tell them whether their work is good enough, and they never fully trust the answer.

### Part 5: Your Work Speaks — Even When You Do Not

Here is something that will become more important as you grow up, but that starts now: **what you make tells people who you are.**

If someone sees a drawing you made, and it is careful and colorful and finished, they learn something about you. If they see a drawing you scribbled in thirty seconds and abandoned, they learn something different. Neither one is a complete picture of who you are — but both are honest signals.

When you build something for someone — a gift, a card, a project — the care you put into it shows. The person who receives it can feel the difference between something made with care and something thrown together. You do not have to explain it. They can see it. They can feel it.

This is the beginning of what grown-ups call **reputation.** Your reputation is what people know about you based on what they have seen you do. Right now, your reputation is mostly with your family — the people who see your work every day. They know whether you are the kind of person who finishes things or leaves them half-done. They know whether you take care of your tools or leave them in a heap. They know whether your work is honest or careless.

As you get older, your reputation will extend to more people — teachers, friends, teammates, eventually employers and communities. But it starts here, in your home, with the small things you make and how you make them. Every careful project, every cleaned tool, every neatly finished workspace is a small deposit in a bank that grows over time.
content.formative_practices:
These are ongoing practices, not one-time activities. They build the habits that IC2.5 develops.

### Practice 1: Tool Care Routine [After Every Work Session]
Every time you use a tool — any tool, for any task — you clean it and put it away before you do anything else. This is non-negotiable. The work is not done until the tools are cared for. For younger students, this means a small set of tools with clear homes. For older students, this expands to include workshop tools, kitchen tools, and art supplies. The habit is the same regardless of scale: use, clean, return. Every time.

### Practice 2: The Finished Work Check [After Every Project]
When you think you are done with a project, stop. Look at it. Ask yourself three questions:
- Is it actually finished, or did I stop because I was tired of working on it?
- Is my workspace clean?
- Are my tools put away?

If the answer to any of these is no, you are not done yet. This check becomes automatic with practice — eventually you will do it without thinking about it.

### Practice 3: Make Something Real [Weekly]
At least once a week, make something with your hands. It does not have to be big or impressive. A drawing done with full attention. A simple woodworking project. A baked good from scratch. A garden bed prepared and planted. The key: it must be done with care, from start to finish, including cleanup. You should be able to look at it when you are done and feel that honest pride.

### Practice 4: Tool Inventory [Weekly]
Once a week, check your tools. Are they all where they belong? Are any dirty, damaged, or missing? If something needs cleaning, clean it. If something needs fixing, fix it or tell your mentor. If something is missing, find it. This weekly check prevents the slow slide from order into chaos that happens when tools are used but never inventoried.

### Practice 5: Show and Tell [Weekly or Biweekly, With Mentor]
Show your mentor something you made this week. Not for praise — for honest conversation. What went well? What was hard? What would you do differently? What are you proudest of? This is not a performance. It is the earliest form of self-assessment: looking at your own work and seeing it honestly.

### Practice 6: Care for Shared Spaces [Daily]
Identify one shared space — the kitchen after a meal, the living room after play, the workshop after a project — and leave it better than you found it. Not just "not worse." Better. This practice extends the tool-care habit to the environment itself and begins building the understanding that spaces you use are your responsibility.

### Practice 7: A Story of a Maker [Biweekly]
Every two weeks, read or hear a story about someone who made things with care — a carpenter, a blacksmith, a potter, a farmer, an artist, an engineer. After the story, discuss: What did they care about? How could you tell they cared? What would their work have looked like if they had not cared? This connects the child's daily practices to a larger tradition of craftsmanship.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Tool Care Is the Anchor

If there is one practice that defines IC2.5-A, it is tool care. This is the concrete, observable, daily behavior that everything else builds from. The child who learns to clean and return every tool after every use is learning, in physical form, the posture of a craftsman: what I use, I take care of. What I borrow, I return in good condition. What helps me work, I do not neglect.

**Your job is to make tool care non-negotiable.** Not sometimes. Not when convenient. Not when you remember to enforce it. Every time, without exception. The child's internal standard will calibrate to whatever standard you actually maintain — not the one you state.

**Practical implementation:**
- Every tool the child uses has a specific home. The child knows where it is. The child returns it there.
- Cleaning happens immediately after use, not "later." Paintbrushes get washed now. The saw gets wiped now. The garden trowel gets knocked clean now. "Later" means never, and the child learns that the standard is not real.
- When the child forgets — and they will forget — the correction is immediate, calm, and consistent. "Your paintbrush is still on the table. Go wash it and put it away." No lecture. No anger. Just the standard, applied.

### What You Model

If you want the child to care for their tools, you must care for yours. If you leave your kitchen knives unwashed, your workshop cluttered, your tools scattered — the child sees this. They learn from what is, not from what you say should be.

This does not require perfection. It requires visible consistency and honest self-correction. If you notice you left your own tools out, say so: "I left my drill on the bench. I need to go put it away." The child sees that the standard applies to everyone, including the adult. This is IC1.3 (integrity) demonstrated in real time.

### Real Work, Not Busywork

IC2.5-A requires that the child actually make things — with real tools, real materials, and real stakes. A crayon drawing counts. A garden bed counts. A batch of cookies counts. A simple woodworking project counts. What does not count is worksheets about craftsmanship, abstract discussions about the importance of tools, or any activity where the child is not physically engaging with materials that can be cared for or wasted.

The work does not need to be complex. It needs to be real. A five-year-old carefully coloring a picture, cleaning their crayons, and putting them back in the box is practicing IC2.5 at exactly the right level. A nine-year-old building a birdhouse, sanding the edges, cleaning the saw and hammer, and sweeping up the sawdust is practicing IC2.5 at their level. The principle scales; the habit is the same.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Tool Care Without Prompting

**Developing well:** The child automatically cleans and returns tools after use. It is part of the workflow, not an afterthought. They may occasionally need a reminder, but the trend is strongly toward independence. When they forget, a single prompt produces immediate action.

**Developing slowly:** The child routinely leaves tools out, dirty, or in the wrong place. Reminders are required after every use. The pattern is not improving over weeks.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does every tool have a clear, specific home the child knows? (If the child does not know where the tool goes, the system is the problem, not the child.) Is the expectation enforced every time? (If you let it slide on Tuesday, you have told the child the standard is optional.) Is the tool set too large for the child's capacity? (Start smaller. Three tools cared for perfectly is better than twenty tools neglected.)

### 2. Material Respect

**Developing well:** The child uses materials intentionally. They take what they need, not more. They do not waste paper, paint, wood, or other supplies habitually. When materials are limited, they adjust rather than demanding more.

**Developing slowly:** The child is habitually wasteful — takes more than needed, damages materials through carelessness, or treats supplies as disposable. They request new materials without using what they have.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child been shown what "enough" looks like? (Some children waste because they genuinely do not know how much they need. Show them.) Is there a consequence for waste? (Not punishment — the natural consequence: "You used all the paint on one project. There is no more paint this week. Next time, use less.") Are you modeling material respect? (If the household is habitually wasteful, the child will be too.)

### 3. Finishing Neatly

**Developing well:** The child's definition of "done" includes cleanup. When they say "I'm finished," the workspace is clean, tools are away, and materials are returned. This has become part of how they think about completion.

**Developing slowly:** The child considers a project "done" the moment the product is complete. Cleanup requires a separate instruction and is treated as an additional task imposed by the adult rather than as part of the work itself.

**Key formation move:** When the child says "I'm done" and the workspace is still messy, do not say "now clean up." Say "Are you done?" and look pointedly at the workspace. Let the child make the connection. If they do not, prompt once: "Is your workspace ready for the next person?" Over time, they will internalize the check.

### 4. Pride in Honest Work

**Developing well:** The child shows genuine satisfaction in work done well. They want to show it — not for praise, but because they are proud. They can distinguish between work they are proud of and work they are not. When asked, they describe what makes good work good with specificity, not just "I like it."

**Developing slowly:** The child is indifferent to the quality of their output. They show no particular satisfaction in completion. Or conversely, they require constant external validation: "Is this good? Do you like it?" without any internal assessment.

**Critical distinction — praise vs. attention:** IC2.5-A does not require you to praise everything the child makes. It requires you to give honest attention. Look at the work. Comment on specific things you see: "These corners are very clean" or "I can see you spent time on this part." If the work is careless, do not pretend otherwise: "This looks rushed. Is this your best work?" Honest attention — not reflexive praise — is what builds the internal compass.

### 5. The Finished Work Check

**Developing well:** The child pauses before declaring a project done and self-assesses. They check: Is it actually finished? Is the workspace clean? Are tools put away? This self-check is becoming habitual.

**Developing slowly:** The child moves on immediately when the product is complete, with no pause for assessment. No self-check occurs.

**How to build this:** Model it yourself. When you finish a task, narrate the check: "Let me see — the food is done, counter is wiped, dishes are in the sink, stove is off. Okay, I'm done." The child sees the process and begins to replicate it.

### 6. Care for Shared Spaces

**Developing well:** The child leaves shared spaces in acceptable or improved condition without being told. After using the kitchen, the counters are wiped. After playing in the living room, items are returned. The habit extends beyond personal tools to the environment.

**Developing slowly:** The child treats shared spaces as someone else's problem. Messes are left. Cleanup only happens when specifically directed.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Praise-Inflation
The greatest threat to IC2.5-A is hollow praise. If every project receives "Great job!" regardless of quality, the child learns that the standard is meaningless and that the adults' assessment is unreliable. The result is a child who neither trusts external feedback nor develops internal standards. Reserve genuine praise for genuinely good work. For everything else, give honest attention and specific feedback.

### Making Cleanup Punitive
If cleanup is framed as punishment — "Since you made this mess, NOW you have to clean it up" — the child learns that cleaning up is a consequence of doing wrong, not a natural part of doing anything. Cleanup should be matter-of-fact, expected, and unremarkable. It is part of the work, like any other step. The tone should be the same as any other instruction, not loaded with frustration or delivered as a consequence.

### Doing It for Them
When the child's cleanup is slow or imperfect, it is faster to do it yourself. Every time you do this, you teach the child that if they are slow enough, someone else will handle it. The habit you are forming requires that the child does the work — slowly, imperfectly, and with your patience. A five-year-old's cleanup will not meet your standards. That is fine. Their effort, consistently applied, is what builds the habit.

### Disconnecting Tools from Work
If tool care is taught as an isolated rule ("always put your crayons away") rather than connected to the work itself ("the crayons are how you make your pictures — taking care of them means your next picture will be better"), the child learns a rule rather than a principle. Rules are followed when enforced and ignored when not. Principles are internalized. Always connect tool care to the work the tool enables.

### Perfection as the Enemy
IC2.5-A is about care, not perfection. A five-year-old's drawing done with genuine attention and care is IC2.5 success, even if it is technically crude. A nine-year-old's birdhouse that is slightly crooked but built with honest effort, sanded smooth, and finished properly is IC2.5 success. The standard is honest effort and complete finishing — not adult-level output from a child.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Tools
**Strong:** Names specific tools. Describes how they care for them with detail: "I wash my paintbrushes in the sink and let them dry, then put them in the cup on my art shelf." Can explain what happens when tools are neglected: "If I don't wash the brush, the paint dries and the brush is ruined."
**Weak:** "I put stuff away." No specifics. Cannot articulate why tool care matters.

### Prompt 2: Something They Are Proud Of
**Strong:** Describes a specific project. Can articulate what made it good: "I sanded all the edges so they were smooth" or "I colored inside the lines and used all the right colors." The pride is connected to specific effort, not just the fact that it exists.
**Weak:** "I made a thing. It was good." No specifics about what made it good or what effort went into it.

### Prompt 3: What "Finished" Means
**Strong:** Includes cleanup and tool care in their definition. "Finished means the thing is done AND my workspace is clean AND my tools are put away." This indicates internalization of the full completion standard.
**Weak:** "Finished means I'm done making it." Cleanup is not part of their concept of done.

### Prompt 4: A Time They Were Careless
**Strong:** Can recall a specific instance with honest self-assessment. Describes what happened as a result of the carelessness and what they would do differently. This shows developing self-awareness.
**Weak:** "I don't remember" or "I'm always careful." Either genuinely cannot recall (the self-awareness is not yet developed) or is unwilling to admit imperfection.

### Prompt 5: Why We Clean Up
**Strong:** Goes beyond "because we're supposed to." Articulates a reason connected to future work, respect for others, or the space itself: "So the space is ready for next time" or "Because someone else might want to use the table" or "Because the tools need to be taken care of."
**Weak:** "Because Mom said so." The rule is known but the reason is not.

### Prompt 6: A Maker from a Story
**Strong:** Names a specific person and describes their care with detail. Can connect the story to their own work: "He was careful like how I try to be careful with my birdhouse."
**Weak:** "Someone made something." No specifics, no connection to their own experience.
